name: Google Shape;102;p13
text:
My research focuses on developing an AI-powered robotic system for stress reduction. As mental health becomes an increasingly important healthcare challenge, our goal is to overcome the limitations of existing stress-monitoring applications, which only detect stress without providing personalized support.
Our system combines real-time stress detection with a socially assistive robot that provides personalized interventions and continuously adapts to each user's physiological profile.

name: TextBox 10
text:
Problem Statement
Mental health disorders are rising worldwide, especially among the youth. Mental health assessments often rely on infrequent clinical visits and self-reported questionnaires, limiting timely identification of changes in mental well-being and adequate care. My research explores how everyday smartphone interactions among the youth can support continuous and privacy-preserving mental health monitoring. In addition, my research explores developing user-centered, explainable and responsible AI technologies to assist clinicians and healthcare providers in providing timely interventions and personalized treatments.

name: TextBox 13
text: Research Title: Human-Centred Explainable Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Bias Mitigation
textPreview: Research Title: Human-Centred Explainable Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Bias Mitigation

name: TextBox 14
text:
Problem Statement:
Many AI systems learn from sensitive user data, but users often do not understand what happens to their data or whether the system treats different groups fairly. Federated learning can help by keeping raw data on users’ devices, but users still need clear explanations about what is protected, what is shared, and whether bias is being checked or reduced.
textPreview: Problem Statement: | Many AI systems learn from sensitive user data, but users often do not understand what happens to their data or whet...

name: TextBox 16
text:
Outcome:
The research aims to support calibrated trust in AI systems by helping users understand what federated learning does, what privacy protections exist, and whether fairness concerns are being addressed.
The outcome could guide the design of more transparent, user-centred, and trustworthy federated learning systems.
textPreview: Outcome: | The research aims to support calibrated trust in AI systems by helping users understand what federated learning does, what pri...

name: TextBox 17
text:
Methodology:
This research will first review existing studies on explainable federated learning to identify gaps.
Then, it will design and evaluate user-friendly explanations that help people understand how federated learning may protect data and how bias may be monitored or mitigated.

name: Text 11
text: Research Title: Designing Adaptive Cybersecurity Interventions Using AI: Effects of Just-in-Time Personalized Nudges on User Security Behavior, Adherence, and Trust

name: Text 13
text: Despite heavy investment in technical defenses, most breaches trace back to human error — phishing clicks, password reuse, ignored updates. Annual training and generic warnings arrive too late, treat every user the same, and give people no way to calibrate trust in AI-driven security guidance.
textPreview: Despite heavy investment in technical defenses, most breaches trace back to human error — phishing clicks, password reuse, ignored update...

name: Text 15
text: This research designs and evaluates an AI-powered Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) system that detects moments of cybersecurity vulnerability and delivers personalized nudges. Lab and longitudinal field studies compare intervention modalities and adaptive vs. static delivery to measure behavior change, habituation, and trust.
textPreview: This research designs and evaluates an AI-powered Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) system that detects moments of cybersecurity...

name: Text 17
text: The research aims to show whether personalized, context-aware nudges improve security behavior adherence over time, and how users calibrate trust in AI-driven guidance. Findings will inform design guidelines for effective, autonomy-preserving cybersecurity coaching systems.

title: Exploring Hate Speech as a Cyber-Threat in Nigerian Languages

name: Content Placeholder 2
text: Exploring Hate Speech as a Cyber-Threat in Nigerian Languages
textPreview: Exploring Hate Speech as a Cyber-Threat in Nigerian Languages |

name: Content Placeholder 2
text:
PROBLEM STATEMENT
Many African nations  are Multi-ethnic/Multi-Religious/Patriarchal in Nature, 
Unchecked can lead to ethnic,  religious and gender-based hate online.
Native languages are vulnerable channel for spreading hate speech.

TECHNOLOGY/STACK
Fine-tune multilingual transformers (XLM-RoBERTa, AfroXLMR, AfriBERTa), Explainable AI, HCI

METHODOLOGY
collect and label native-language corpora; 
fine-tune multilingual transformers (XLM-RoBERTa, AfroXLMR, AfriBERTa) to detect hate 
integrate explainable AI to explain the model's decision to users.

IMPACT
Reduced cases of hate-motivated violence (real and virtual)
Blueprint for protecting other low-resource languages

textPreview: Topic: A Multimodal Adaptive System for Detection and Prevention of Loneliness Among Immigrants in Canada

name: TextBox 23
text: Loneliness affects over 40% of Canadians, with immigrants disproportionately impacted due to cultural displacement and social disconnection. In Canada, 1 in 3 immigrants experience chronic loneliness, linked to depression, anxiety, and reduced economic participation. Globally, WHO declared loneliness a public health epidemic in 2023, affecting 1 in 4 adults yet no real-time persuasive system exists to detect and intervene proactively.

name: Text 9
text: My Research: Can AI Silently Judge You By Your Dialect?
textPreview: My Research: Can AI Silently Judge You By Your Dialect?

name: Text 13
text: AI models like ChatGPT silently judge users by how they write, not just what they say (Similar to humans!)
textPreview: AI models like ChatGPT silently judge users by how they write, not just what they say (Similar to humans!)

name: Text 17

name: Text 21

textPreview: Open-weight language models running in our Lab GPU

name: Text 25
text: Disambiguates the hidden bias in AI for high-stakes domains (mental health, housing, etc.) and their implications on Contextual Privacy Violation
textPreview: Disambiguates the hidden bias in AI for high-stakes domains (mental health, housing, etc.) and their implications on Contextual Privacy V...

text:
Hi everyone, my name is Chowdhury Mohammad Abdullah. I'm a second-year Master's student in Computer Science at Dalhousie, supervised by Dr. Rita Orji.

My research looks at a problem most people don't realize exists: AI models like ChatGPT don't just answer your questions, they also silently judge you based on how you write. Specifically, certain English dialects get quietly associated with lower competence or trustworthiness inside the model, even though nothing about the dialect actually reflects the person's ability. This matters because it's an invisible, undisclosed form of profiling, and it becomes a real privacy and fairness risk if these AI systems are ever used to help with high-stakes decisions, like screening job applicants or housing applications.

To study this, I work with open-source language models such as Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and BERT, using Python and Dalhousie's GPU cluster. My method adapts a classic social-science technique called the matched-guise test: I take the same message and rewrite it in Standard English and in two Nigerian English varieties, then measure the bias the model shows underneath its polished, surface-level responses.

My first results have been accepted at AIES 2026, a leading international AI-ethics conference. My long-term goal is to build a framework that can audit AI systems for this kind of hidden bias before they're trusted with real, high-stakes decisions.

Thank you.

text:
Hi everyone, my name is Chowdhury Mohammad Abdullah. I'm a second-year Master's student in Computer Science at Dalhousie, supervised by Dr. Rita Orji.

My research looks at a problem most people don't realize exists: AI models like ChatGPT don't just answer your questions, they also silently judge you based on how you write. Specifically, certain English dialects get quietly associated with lower competence or trustworthiness inside the model, even though nothing about the dialect actually reflects the person's ability. This matters because it's an invisible, undisclosed form of profiling, and it becomes a real privacy and fairness risk if these AI systems are ever used to help with high-stakes decisions, like screening job applicants or housing applications.

To study this, I work with open-source language models such as Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and BERT, using Python and Dalhousie's GPU cluster. My method adapts a classic social-science technique called the matched-guise test: I take the same message and rewrite it in Standard English and in two Nigerian English varieties, then measure the bias the model shows underneath its polished, surface-level responses.

My first results have been accepted at AIES 2026, a leading international AI-ethics conference. My long-term goal is to build a framework that can audit AI systems for this kind of hidden bias before they're trusted with real, high-stakes decisions.

Thank you.

name: Text 49
text: Digital health technology promises care that adapts to the individual, and delivering that requires sensing the person, understanding them, and acting on what is understood. Existing systems build all of this from scratch, so the same work is repeated each time and what reaches the patient remains one fixed product calibrated to an average user.

name: Text 52
text: The foundation is designed as five layers that collect sensor data, clean it, model the person, select a behaviour change strategy, and deliver the intervention. That foundation will be employed and validated in systems that support cardimetabolic conditions (e.g., hypertension, diabetes etc.)

Across different health domains, care that adapts to individuals therefore becomes accessible and scalable to diverse populations.

name: TextBox 8
text:
1. Research Problem
Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is one of the fastest-growing chronic diseases worldwide, with lifestyle behaviours such as physical inactivity, poor sleep, prolonged sedentary behaviour, and chronic stress significantly increasing disease risk. Current wearable health systems mainly monitor user behaviour but rarely provide personalized, privacy-preserving interventions. This research proposes an Edge AI and Federated Learning framework to deliver real-time adaptive support for Type 2 Diabetes prevention.
textPreview: 1. Research Problem | Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is one of the fastest-growing chronic diseases worldwide, with lifestyle behaviours such as p...

name: TextBox 1
text:
2. System Architecture
The proposed framework transforms passive wearable sensing into real-time, personalized behavioural interventions through an end-to-end privacy-preserving AI pipeline. Physiological data are captured from wearable devices, processed locally using Edge AI, and interpreted by a JITAI engine to deliver adaptive recommendations. Federated Learning continuously refines the AI model without transmitting sensitive health data.
Wearable Sensors → Health Connect → Flutter App → Edge AI (TensorFlow Lite) → JITAI Engine → Federated Learning (Flower)
textPreview: 2. System Architecture | The proposed framework transforms passive wearable sensing into real-time, personalized behavioural intervention...

name: TextBox 4
text:
3. Research Methodology
The research follows a four-phase methodology to design, develop, and evaluate a privacy-preserving mobile health framework.
Phase 1 – Systematic Literature Review:
 A PRISMA 2020-guided SLR (161 studies) identified research gaps and informed the framework design.
Phase 2 – Co-design:
 Stakeholder feedback  incorporation to refine system requirements, user interfaces, and intervention strategies.
Phase 3 – System Development:
 Developing an Edge AI-enabled Flutter application by integrating wearable sensing, Federated Learning, and a JITAI engine.
Phase 4 – Pilot Evaluation:
 The prototype will be assessed for usability, feasibility, personalization, and user engagement in real-world settings
textPreview: 3. Research Methodology | The research follows a four-phase methodology to design, develop, and evaluate a privacy-preserving mobile heal...

name: TextBox 10
text:
4. Expected Contributions
 A comprehensive evidence synthesis of AI-enabled wearable healthcare systems through a PRISMA-based Systematic Literature Review.
A novel privacy-preserving framework integrating Edge AI, Federated Learning, wearable sensing, and Just-In-Time Adaptive Interventions for Type 2 Diabetes prevention.
A human-centred co-design methodology that incorporates stakeholderfeedback into system development.
A personalized intervention engine capable of delivering adaptive behavioural recommendations using real-time physiological and contextual information.
textPreview: 4. Expected Contributions |  A comprehensive evidence synthesis of AI-enabled wearable healthcare systems through a PRISMA-based Systemat...

name: TextBox 8
text:
Problem Statement
Current interactive systems struggle to model physical movement reliably across heterogeneous sensors.
Vision-only and isolated wearable approaches lack synchronized, standardized signals.
This limits accurate analysis of posture, load symmetry, and physical exertion.

name: TextBox 1
text:
Methodology & Tech Stack
Camera + YOLO: Body-pose estimation and object detection.
ESP32 + IMU: Precise motion, orientation, and stability telemetry.
Garmin Venu 3: Real-time heart-rate and physiological context.
Pipeline: Synchronizes and exports normalized JSON/JSONL features.
